speaker: Peter
en: Hi, Shona.

speaker: Shona
en: Hi, Peter.

speaker: Shona
en: Have you made up your mind about which internship programme to apply for?

speaker: Peter
en: Yes, pretty much.

speaker: Shona
en: I'm entirely at a loss.

speaker: Shona
en: I mean, there are so many good ones.

speaker: Shona
en: Can you give me some tips?

speaker: Peter
en: Sure.

speaker: Shona
en: What is your top priority when weighing up these programmes?

speaker: Peter
en: Well, I guess I'd go for ones that can help me with the skill set I lack for my future career.

speaker: Peter
en: I still need a lot of practical training in dealing with statistics.

speaker: Peter
en: But keep in mind that you don't want to set your goal too grand, like drawing up a business plan.

speaker: Peter
en: You might end up nowhere.

speaker: Shona
en: I see.

speaker: Shona
en: So, I want to learn how to organise ideas and present them.

speaker: Peter
en: Good.

speaker: Peter
en: Now you've nailed step one.

speaker: Shona
en: Now what?

speaker: Peter
en: You must also consider your strengths for a suitable internship programme.

speaker: Peter
en: Otherwise, you might get rejected or end up with one that you are incapable of doing.

speaker: Shona
en: But I have no idea what I'm good at.

speaker: Peter
en: Communicate with people around you or whom you've worked with.

speaker: Peter
en: That's what I did.

speaker: Peter
en: I thought I was a good team leader, but many people I've worked with for various projects mentioned something else.

speaker: Peter
en: They admire that I could work around the clock to finish projects on time.

speaker: Peter
en: And indeed, I'm more focused and perform better when I'm pushed for time.

speaker: Shona
en: Good point.

speaker: Shona
en: So what do you plan to do next?

speaker: Peter
en: I'll visit the careers officer to help me with the school application form.

speaker: Peter
en: You know, the one we have to submit before applying to companies.

speaker: Shona
en: But it is unnecessary to fill it in.

speaker: Shona
en: It's only optional.

speaker: Peter
en: Really?

speaker: Peter
en: I'm glad I heard from you.

speaker: Peter
en: Then I'll ask the careers officer to look at my personal statement and give me some tips on polishing it up.

speaker: Shona
en: Don't you need help with your CV?

speaker: Peter
en: No, I'm good.

speaker: Peter
en: I have already learned to write it in class.

speaker: Shona
en: That's good.

speaker: Shona
en: So what kind of company did you choose?

speaker: Peter
en: I've decided to apply for an IT company.

speaker: Shona
en: Why is that?

speaker: Peter
en: Well, even though I've learned how the IT industry developed in the past from different lectures, I know nothing about its latest trends.

speaker: Peter
en: I'm prepared to work in an IT company and fill in the information gap.

speaker: Shona
en: I see.

speaker: Shona
en: What do you plan to do next term?

speaker: Peter
en: I think I'll stick to most of the optional modules I've chosen.

speaker: Peter
en: But regarding the language sessions, I'm doing poorly in Japanese.

speaker: Peter
en: I'd better drop it before wasting more time.

speaker: Peter
en: I think I'll learn Spanish instead.

speaker: Shona
en: Oh, I learned Spanish for a year.

speaker: Shona
en: I think you'll find it interesting.

speaker: Shona
en: Do you know what job opportunities these companies offer interns?

speaker: Peter
en: I've searched online and browsed some of the schemes.

speaker: Peter
en: Do you know SAIC?

speaker: Shona
en: Yes.

speaker: Shona
en: I heard they offer internship positions dealing with customer interaction management.

speaker: Shona
en: But I'm not quite sure what that means.

speaker: Peter
en: Well, the position offers the chance to become fully aware of customer value and keep in touch with customers to keep them highly engaged and satisfied.

speaker: Peter
en: These include activities such as PR, communications, marketing and customer service.

speaker: Shona
en: Sounds like loads of work to do.

speaker: Shona
en: What about Gemini Technologies?

speaker: Peter
en: It's an IT consulting company.

speaker: Peter
en: Although many employees say it's stressful working there, I want to apply for this one.

speaker: Shona
en: Why is that?

speaker: Peter
en: Actually, the company provides service to improve software architecture.

speaker: Peter
en: But that's pretty demanding as a team of software engineers has to provide detailed analysis and suggestions for required changes before the deadline.

speaker: Peter
en: As an observer and participant of a particular project, an intern can learn how to work to a strict deadline.

speaker: Shona
en: That's not my area of expertise.

speaker: Shona
en: But I'm sure you'll excel at this.

speaker: Peter
en: I certainly do hope so.

speaker: Peter
en: Have you checked out Deloitte?

speaker: Shona
en: Of course.

speaker: Shona
en: That's a renowned accounting firm, and thus its internships offer excellent learning experiences in this regard.

speaker: Shona
en: You know, things like examining a company's financial statements - the income statement, balance sheet and cash flow statement.

speaker: Shona
en: I want to give it a try.

speaker: Shona
en: But I might get rejected, so I need a backup plan.

speaker: Shona
en: Have you read anything about Kearney?

speaker: Peter
en: Yes.

speaker: Peter
en: It is another consulting firm.

speaker: Peter
en: It provides opportunities to reach out to other business partners and pay attention to networking.

speaker: Shona
en: Well, that sounds interesting.

speaker: Peter
en: I'm thinking about Vortex.

speaker: Peter
en: But I haven't got anything useful from its website.

speaker: Shona
en: I worked there last summer.

speaker: Shona
en: It's an international company with headquarters in Spain, so most of the managers here speak Spanish.

speaker: Shona
en: Thanks to the internship experience, I became fluent in it.

speaker: Peter
en: Right.

speaker: Peter
en: That's not for me.
